text: Design and additive manufacturing of metamaterials with tailored microstructure offers unprecedented opportunities to tailor new mechanical properties. These are often described through the conventional stress–strain response, i.e., the material’s quasi-static behaviour. While significant advances have been reported, existing inverse design methods are limited in terms of ability to tailor complex and multi-functional behaviours, due to the difficulties in achieving multi-objective optimization, non-linearity, and process-dependent defects, which are inherent to the additive manufacturing process. In this work, we develop a fast inverse design strategy that leverages generative machine learning and the corresponding desktop additive manufacturing technology to design and 3D print nearly any uniaxial compressive stress–strain profile, while accommodating the printing-induced structural defects. Our results show that full mechanical tailoring is feasible with less than 10% loss of target performance and nearly 90% measurement consistency. Our method thus enables a rapid transition from desired mechanical behaviour to material realization, eliminating the need for lengthy trial-and-error design and manufacturing iterations.
